stem:
A 34-year-old G2P1 at 32 weeks gestation is admitted with a severe occipital headache and visual blurring. Her blood pressure on arrival is 178/118 mmHg confirmed on a second reading 15 minutes later, with proteinuria 4+ on urine dipstick. She is not asthmatic. She has no past cardiovascular history and her booking blood pressure was 118/72 mmHg. Foetal CTG is reactive with normal variability. Antenatal corticosteroids and IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis have been commenced by the obstetric registrar.

The consultant asks you to start an acute antihypertensive while preparations for delivery are made.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 92 /min, blood pressure 178/118 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.6 degrees Celsius, foetal heart rate 142 /min reactive
data_table.Examination: alert, hyperreflexic 3+ knee jerks, no clonus, no focal neurology, fundoscopy normal, no asthma history
data_table.Investigations: platelets 158 x10^9/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L, ALT 38 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urine protein creatinine ratio 280 mg/mmol, magnesium infusion running at 1 g/hr after 4 g loading dose

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line antihypertensive?

options.text: Sublingual immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg crushed and placed under the tongue
options.rationale: Sublingual immediate-release nifedipine causes precipitous unpredictable blood pressure drops with risk of placental hypoperfusion and is contraindicated in pregnancy hypertension. SOMANZ avoids this route. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Oral methyldopa 250 mg three times daily titrated upwards over 48 hours
options.rationale: Methyldopa has onset over 24 to 48 hours and is used for chronic, mild-to-moderate gestational hypertension. It cannot acutely control severe hypertension. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol 20 mg slow bolus over 2 minutes repeated every 10 minutes
options.rationale: SOMANZ lists IV labetalol, IV hydralazine and oral immediate-release nifedipine as first-line acute agents for severe pregnancy hypertension. With no asthma contraindication, labetalol is preferred for its predictable dose-response, lower risk of reflex tachycardia than hydralazine, and infusion availability for ongoing control. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg as a bolus to reduce circulating intravascular volume
options.rationale: Diuretics worsen the intravascular volume depletion that is characteristic of pre-eclampsia and are not first-line antihypertensives. They are reserved for pulmonary oedema. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

options.text: Oral atenolol 50 mg as a single oral loading dose then daily thereafter
options.rationale: Atenolol is avoided in pregnancy because of association with foetal growth restriction and is not used for acute control of severe BP. (Source: SOMANZ pre-eclampsia guideline)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ identifies IV labetalol, IV hydralazine and oral immediate-release nifedipine as first-line for acute severe BP in pre-eclampsia. Labetalol is preferred when there is no asthma or significant bradycardia and offers infusion-based ongoing control. Sublingual nifedipine, diuretics, ACE inhibitors and atenolol are contraindicated or inappropriate. The target is to bring BP under 160/110 mmHg without dropping it precipitously, balancing maternal cerebrovascular risk against placental perfusion.
key_points: First-line acute agents (SOMANZ): IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, oral IR nifedipine
Avoid sublingual nifedipine, diuretics, atenolol, ACE inhibitors
Target BP under 160/110 without precipitous drop
Labetalol preferred when no asthma; hydralazine for asthma; oral IR nifedipine if no IV access
Magnesium for seizure prophylaxis does not lower BP
explanation.pearls: The 2014 SOMANZ guideline is still the operative document in most AU jurisdictions - the only routine substitution to watch is that labetalol IV is widely available but the older oral hydralazine has been phased out.

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P1 with a spontaneous monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy is referred at 19 weeks gestation after a routine fortnightly ultrasound. She is otherwise well, has no contractions and her cervix is closed and 38 mm long on transvaginal scan. Both twins are alive with normal anatomy. There is no maternal heart disease.

The sonographer's report describes polyhydramnios in twin A with deepest vertical pocket 9.2 cm and oligohydramnios in twin B with deepest vertical pocket 1.6 cm. Twin B's bladder is not visualised after 60 minutes. Umbilical artery Doppler in twin B shows persistent end-diastolic flow. The estimated weight discordance is 18 percent.

data_table.Examination: soft non-tender uterus consistent with 22 weeks size, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: TVUS cervix 38 mm closed; twin A DVP 9.2 cm with visible bladder; twin B DVP 1.6 cm with absent bladder visualisation; UA Doppler in twin B with present end-diastolic flow; both twins alive with normal anatomy; 18% weight discordance

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Fortnightly ultrasound surveillance with elective delivery at 36 weeks
options.rationale: This is Quintero stage II TTTS (oligo-polyhydramnios with absent bladder visualisation in donor). Expectant management has poor outcomes with high perinatal mortality. Intervention is recommended. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline)

options.text: Serial amnioreduction from the polyhydramnic sac on a fortnightly schedule
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is inferior to fetoscopic laser ablation for Quintero stage 2 to 4 TTTS at less than 26 weeks per the Eurofoetus trial, with worse neurological outcomes. It is now reserved for cases where laser is unavailable or contraindicated. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline)

options.text: Urgent foetal medicine referral for fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses
options.rationale: Quintero stage 2 or higher TTTS before 26 weeks is an indication for fetoscopic laser ablation, the AU and international standard of care. The Eurofoetus trial demonstrated improved survival of at least one twin and lower neurological morbidity compared with amnioreduction. Referral is time-sensitive. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline)

options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin to maximise survival of the recipient
options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for advanced TTTS with imminent demise of one twin or when laser is unavailable. It is not first-line for Quintero stage II. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline)

options.text: Antenatal corticosteroids followed by emergency caesarean delivery at 24 weeks
options.rationale: Delivery at 19 to 24 weeks confers extreme prematurity morbidity and is not the first option when in utero laser therapy is available. (Source: ISUOG monochorionic twin guideline)

explanation.summary: TTTS arises in monochorionic placentas via unbalanced inter-twin vascular anastomoses, producing a recipient with polyhydramnios and a donor with oligohydramnios. The Quintero staging system steps from oligo-polyhydramnios alone (I), through absent donor bladder (II), abnormal Doppler (III), hydrops (IV), to demise (V). Quintero stage 2 or higher before 26 weeks is an indication for urgent fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses at a foetal medicine centre. Laser improves at-least-one-twin survival to around 75 percent and reduces neurological morbidity compared with amnioreduction.
key_points: TTTS occurs in MCDA twins via shared placental anastomoses
Quintero staging: I oligo-poly, II absent bladder, III Doppler, IV hydrops, V demise
Stage 2 or more before 26 weeks: refer for fetoscopic laser
Eurofoetus trial: laser superior to amnioreduction for stage 2 to 4
Post-laser surveillance for TAPS (twin anaemia-polycythaemia sequence)
explanation.pearls: MCDA twins require fortnightly scans from 16 weeks because TTTS evolves over days - the screening interval, not the staging itself, is the lever that catches it in time.

subtopic_detail: Vasa praevia - antenatal corticosteroids and timing of caesarean
stem:
A 31-year-old G3P2 has a routine 32-week morphology follow-up ultrasound for low-lying placenta noted at 20 weeks. The placenta is now confirmed posterior and clear of the internal os. However, the sonographer identifies a velamentous cord insertion into a succenturiate lobe with foetal vessels crossing within 2 cm of the internal os, confirmed on transvaginal colour Doppler. The patient is asymptomatic with no bleeding and no contractions. Her cervix measures 35 mm with no funnelling.

She has had two previous uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. Her booking BMI was 28 and antenatal screens are otherwise unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: vital signs within normal limits, foetal heart rate 144 /min reactive
data_table.Examination: soft non-tender uterus consistent with dates, no PV bleeding, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: posterior placenta clear of os, velamentous cord into succenturiate lobe with foetal vessels overlying the internal os, cervix 35 mm no funnelling; CTG reactive; FBC and group/screen normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with weekly outpatient ultrasound and aim for vaginal delivery at term
options.rationale: Vasa praevia carries a foetal mortality near 60 percent if labour or rupture of membranes occurs without elective caesarean. Outpatient surveillance with planned vaginal delivery is dangerous. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Continuous CTG monitoring with planned elective vaginal delivery at 39 weeks under supervision
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated. Membrane rupture risks foetal exsanguination from torn vasa praevia vessels in seconds. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Outpatient observation with planned induction of labour at 37 weeks under continuous monitoring
options.rationale: Induction risks membrane rupture before delivery and is contraindicated. RANZCOG recommends elective caesarean, not induction. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Emergency caesarean section today under general anaesthesia in next available theatre
options.rationale: She is asymptomatic with no foetal compromise, no PV bleeding and no contractions. Elective caesarean at 34 to 35 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids gives the best balance of prematurity risk and the risk of unplanned membrane rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Admit for inpatient surveillance, give antenatal corticosteroids and plan elective caesarean at 34 to 35 weeks
options.rationale: RANZCOG recommends inpatient admission from around 32 weeks, antenatal corticosteroids around 32 to 34 weeks, and elective caesarean at 34 to 35 weeks before spontaneous labour risk peaks. This balances iatrogenic prematurity against the catastrophic risk of vessel rupture at labour or membrane rupture. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is foetal vessels running through membranes within 2 cm of the internal os, often associated with velamentous cord insertion or succenturiate lobe. Foetal mortality at labour or membrane rupture is around 60 percent without antenatal diagnosis. The RANZCOG management pathway is antenatal diagnosis by transvaginal Doppler, admission around 32 weeks for proximity to theatre, antenatal corticosteroids at 32 to 34 weeks, and elective caesarean at 34 to 35 weeks. Vaginal delivery and induction are contraindicated.
key_points: Vasa praevia: foetal vessels within 2 cm of internal os
Often with velamentous cord or succenturiate lobe
Mortality near 60% if labour or ROM occurs without diagnosis
Admit 32 weeks, steroids 32 to 34 weeks, elective LSCS 34 to 35 weeks
Avoid digital cervical exams once diagnosed
explanation.pearls: The 'silver bullet' sign on transvaginal Doppler - a colour-flow line crossing the internal os that does not move with maternal Valsalva - distinguishes vasa praevia from a free-floating cord (which does move).

sources.label: RCOG Green-top 27b: Placenta praevia and vasa praevia

subtopic_detail: HELLP with abruption and foetal distress - delivery decision
stem:
A 30-year-old G3P2 at 34 weeks and 2 days is brought to the obstetric assessment unit with severe upper abdominal pain and reduced foetal movements over the last 90 minutes. She has a known diagnosis of pre-eclampsia, on labetalol 200 mg three times daily and a magnesium sulfate infusion was started 30 minutes ago at the referring hospital. She has had no previous caesarean. Her booking BMI was 24.

On arrival she is tender across the uterus with a firm, woody feel and ongoing dark PV bleeding estimated at 400 mL in the past hour. The continuous CTG shows a baseline of 90 /min with reduced variability and recurrent late decelerations after every contraction. Her cervix is 2 cm dilated and uneffaced on a single examination. Anaesthetics, neonatal team and theatre are mobilised.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 122 /min, blood pressure 172/108 mmHg, respiratory rate 24 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, foetal heart rate baseline 90 /min with recurrent late decelerations
data_table.Examination: uterus tender, firm and woody, ongoing dark PV bleeding 400 mL in last hour, cervix 2 cm uneffaced on one exam
data_table.Investigations: platelets 38 x10^9/L (150 to 400), ALT 480 U/L (less than 40), AST 510 U/L (less than 40), LDH 920 U/L (140 to 280), fibrinogen 1.4 g/L (2 to 4), INR 1.6, haptoglobin 0.05 g/L, schistocytes on film; group O Rh-negative, 4 units packed red cells in fridge

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Emergency caesarean section under general anaesthesia with massive transfusion protocol activation and ongoing magnesium
options.rationale: She has HELLP (platelets 38, ALT 480, LDH 920, haptoglobin low, schistocytes) plus suspected placental abruption with foetal compromise (baseline 90 with recurrent lates) and severe maternal hypertension. Platelets of 38 contraindicate neuraxial anaesthesia. Emergency LSCS under GA with MTP activation, ongoing magnesium for seizure prophylaxis, and acute BP control is the only safe pathway. (Source: SOMANZ HELLP and pre-eclampsia)

options.text: Induce labour with oxytocin and aim for vaginal delivery once cervix favourable, with continuous foetal monitoring
options.rationale: Foetal bradycardia at 90 with recurrent lates is not survivable through prolonged labour, and uterine activity worsens abruption. Time to vaginal delivery is too long. (Source: RANZCOG: Placental abruption)

options.text: Transfuse platelets to above 75 x10^9/L then proceed to caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia
options.rationale: Awaiting platelet transfusion delays delivery in a foetus with bradycardia. Neuraxial anaesthesia is generally avoided when platelets are under 70 to 80 due to spinal haematoma risk. General anaesthesia is correct here. (Source: SOMANZ HELLP and pre-eclampsia)

options.text: Continue expectant management with steroids for foetal lung maturity for 48 hours before delivery
options.rationale: Foetal bradycardia and abruption-related coagulopathy mandate delivery now. Steroids cannot be given a 48-hour window in this scenario. (Source: SOMANZ HELLP and pre-eclampsia)

options.text: Administer high-dose dexamethasone 10 mg IV every 6 hours for HELLP with reassessment in 12 hours
options.rationale: Steroids have not been shown to alter maternal HELLP outcomes and delaying delivery in foetal compromise with abruption is unsafe. Delivery is the definitive treatment. (Source: SOMANZ HELLP and pre-eclampsia)

explanation.summary: HELLP plus placental abruption plus foetal bradycardia in a pre-viable cervix is an emergency delivery indication. Maternal stabilisation (BP control, magnesium, blood products) runs in parallel with not before delivery. Platelets under 70 to 80 x10^9/L generally preclude neuraxial anaesthesia, mandating GA. The MTP threshold is triggered by ongoing blood loss with abnormal coagulation. Steroids do not change maternal HELLP outcomes and should not delay delivery.
key_points: HELLP + abruption + foetal compromise = emergency LSCS
Platelets under 70 to 80 contraindicate neuraxial anaesthesia (use GA)
Activate MTP, continue magnesium, control BP
Steroids do not alter maternal HELLP outcome; do not delay delivery
Anti-D for Rh-negative woman post-delivery
explanation.pearls: The maternal coagulopathy in abruption is consumptive (DIC) so even normal pre-event platelets and fibrinogen drop rapidly - check fibrinogen every 30 to 60 minutes intra-op, not just on admission.

stem:
A 28-year-old primigravid woman at 14 weeks gestation presents to her GP with low mood, anhedonia, early morning waking, poor appetite with no weight gain so far in pregnancy and passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. She meets criteria for a severe major depressive episode on PHQ-9 of 22. She has no past psychiatric history, no substance use and no thyroid disease. She has not tried an antidepressant before. Her partner is supportive and she has agreed to weekly perinatal mental health follow-up.

She is willing to start medication after discussing risks and benefits and asks which antidepressant is safest in pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: appears flat affect, congruent mood, no psychotic features, no thought of self-harm beyond passive ideation
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 22 (severe), Edinburgh PND scale 18, TSH 1.6 mIU/L, no recent illicit drug use

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial antidepressant?

options.text: Paroxetine 20 mg daily, titrated weekly to therapeutic effect
options.rationale: Paroxetine is associated with a small increased risk of foetal cardiac defects in first-trimester exposure and is generally avoided as the initial agent in pregnancy when alternatives exist. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg daily, titrated weekly to therapeutic effect
options.rationale: Sertraline has the largest safety dataset in pregnancy and lactation, with lower placental transfer than other SSRIs. RANZCP perinatal guidelines list sertraline as preferred for new initiation in pregnancy when SSRI therapy is indicated. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg daily, titrated weekly to therapeutic effect
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and high placental transfer, with greater neonatal adaptation syndrome and persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn signal compared with sertraline. Reserved for women already stable on it pre-pregnancy. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg daily, titrated weekly to therapeutic effect
options.rationale: Venlafaxine is an SNRI with more cardiovascular adverse effects and limited safety data in pregnancy. Reserved for treatment-resistant cases. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)

options.text: Mirtazapine 30 mg nocte, titrated weekly to therapeutic effect
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has weaker pregnancy safety data than sertraline. It may be considered for prominent insomnia or poor appetite when SSRI is not tolerated, but is not first-line for a new initiation. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe major depressive episode in pregnancy with suicidal ideation requires pharmacological treatment along with weekly perinatal mental health review. RANZCP and AU Therapeutic Guidelines recommend sertraline as the preferred SSRI for new initiation due to the largest safety dataset, lowest placental transfer and compatibility with breastfeeding. Paroxetine is avoided due to cardiac signal, fluoxetine due to long half-life and neonatal adaptation, and SNRIs reserved for resistance. Untreated severe depression carries higher foetal and neonatal risk than sertraline.
key_points: Sertraline preferred new SSRI in pregnancy (largest safety dataset)
Paroxetine avoided (small first-trimester cardiac signal)
Fluoxetine reserved for women already stable on it
Untreated severe depression poses real risk to mother and foetus
Weekly perinatal mental health follow-up essential
explanation.pearls: The 'safest antidepressant' framing the patient asks about is itself worth a reflective conversation - untreated severe depression has prematurity, low birthweight and suicide outcomes that frame the comparison.

stem:
A 48-year-old man with bipolar I disorder on lithium carbonate 1000 mg nocte for 9 years presents with a 6-month history of polyuria and polydipsia. He drinks 6 litres of fluid daily and rises 4 times at night to void. His mood has been stable for years and his most recent lithium level 2 weeks ago was 0.7 mmol/L. He is not diabetic, his fasting glucose is 5.2 mmol/L and HbA1c is 5.5 percent. There is no family history of polyuria.

A random urine osmolality after 4 hours of fluid restriction is 180 mOsm/kg with paired serum osmolality 298 mOsm/kg. Following 4 micrograms IM desmopressin urine osmolality 60 minutes later is 195 mOsm/kg.

data_table.Examination: no oedema, no postural BP drop, mucous membranes moist
data_table.Investigations: sodium 144 mmol/L (135 to 145), potassium 4.2 mmol/L (3.5 to 5.0), creatinine 96 micromol/L (60 to 110), eGFR 76 mL/min/1.73m^2, fasting glucose 5.2 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.5%, urine osmolality post fluid restriction 180 mOsm/kg, post desmopressin urine osmolality 195 mOsm/kg, serum osmolality 298 mOsm/kg, lithium level 0.7 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Stop lithium immediately and switch to sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium discontinuation in stable bipolar I disorder carries a high relapse risk including manic episodes. Valproate is teratogenic and not interchangeable; switching requires careful planning, not immediate substitution. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Guideline)

options.text: Increase the lithium dose to achieve a higher therapeutic serum level
options.rationale: His level is therapeutic at 0.7 mmol/L. Increasing lithium worsens the renal tubular toxicity driving the nephrogenic DI. (Source: AMH Lithium Monograph)

options.text: Reduce lithium to the minimum effective dose and add amiloride 5 mg daily
options.rationale: The water deprivation plus desmopressin test confirms nephrogenic DI (no concentration after desmopressin). Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI is reduced by lowering lithium exposure and by amiloride, which blocks the apical epithelial sodium channel in the collecting duct and reduces lithium entry into the principal cells. Mood and renal monitoring continue. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Guideline)

options.text: Add hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily for paradoxical antidiuretic effect
options.rationale: Thiazides do have a paradoxical antidiuretic effect in DI but they reduce lithium clearance and predictably raise lithium levels, increasing toxicity risk. Amiloride is preferred in lithium-induced DI for this reason. (Source: AMH Lithium Monograph)

options.text: Commence high-dose intranasal desmopressin 20 micrograms twice daily
options.rationale: Desmopressin is the treatment for central DI. In nephrogenic DI the kidney does not respond to vasopressin, which the water deprivation test has demonstrated. Desmopressin is ineffective. (Source: AMH Lithium Monograph)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus by downregulating aquaporin-2 in the collecting duct, producing polyuria and polydipsia. Diagnosis is by water deprivation with failure to concentrate, then non-response to desmopressin distinguishes nephrogenic from central. Management balances mood stability and renal protection: lower lithium to minimum effective dose, add amiloride to block lithium tubular entry, and avoid thiazides which raise lithium levels. Hydration and salt intake should be maintained; ACE inhibitors and NSAIDs are minimised because they reduce lithium clearance.
key_points: Lithium NDI: failure to concentrate urine after water deprivation and after desmopressin
Lower lithium dose to minimum effective level
Amiloride blocks ENaC and reduces lithium tubular entry
Avoid thiazides (raise lithium level)
Long-term: monitor eGFR yearly; refer nephrology if eGFR falls
explanation.pearls: Amiloride is the under-recognised treatment - it both reduces polyuria and slows progression of lithium nephropathy, which is why nephrology may add it early even when polyuria is mild.
